text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY PROGRESS NOTE
Genitourinary Oncology Clinic

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW/RESIDENT: [Fellow]
PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), right kidney, pT3b pN1 M1, Fuhrman grade 4, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation
CURRENT TREATMENT: Cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily (second-line)
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up / Restaging Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old Nigerian-American woman, retired nurse, presenting for her scheduled restaging assessment and medical oncology follow-up. She is now approximately 9 months into second-line therapy with cabozantinib and approximately 18 months from her original diagnosis of metastatic ccRCC. She reports doing reasonably well overall, with significantly improved back pain, and continues on cabozantinib 40 mg daily.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed with stage IV ccRCC approximately 18 months ago, presenting with a large right renal mass (14.2 cm, T3b with right renal vein tumor thrombus), a single right retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis (2.4 cm, with extranodal extension), and bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.8 cm right lower lobe). Fuhrman nuclear grade 4 with approximately 20% sarcomatoid differentiation was confirmed on both pre-surgical biopsy and definitive surgical pathology. Tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx-equivalent panel) demonstrated VHL p.R200* with concurrent 3p LOH, PBRM1 nonsense mutation, SETD2 frameshift, TP53 p.R175H, and notably high-level MET amplification (copy number 8.2), as well as a subclonal mTOR activating variant. MSS, TMB-low (3.1 mut/Mb), PD-L1 CPS 12. IMDC intermediate-risk.

She underwent upfront cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (R0 resection, pT3b pN1) approximately 18 months ago. Postoperative course was complicated by a brief ileus (CTCAE Grade 2), which resolved conservatively.

Approximately 6 weeks post-nephrectomy she was started on first-line ipilimumab (ipi) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (nivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks per CheckMate 214, chosen given sarcomatoid differentiation (a subgroup with pre-specified enhanced benefit in that trial) and her IMDC intermediate-risk classification. After cycle 2, she developed immune-related Grade 2 colitis requiring oral prednisone taper; ipilimumab was permanently discontinued at that point, and nivolumab was continued as maintenance after steroid taper completion. She also developed subclinical immune-related hypothyroidism (mildly elevated TSH with low-normal free T4), for which levothyroxine was initiated and subsequently dose-titrated.

Imaging at 3 months on immunotherapy showed a mixed response: the dominant right lower lobe (RLL) nodule increased slightly (1.8 cm → 2.1 cm), raising the question of pseudoprogression, while the other two target lesions regressed. Confirmatory imaging at 6 months demonstrated unambiguous progression of the dominant RLL lesion (2.6 cm), two new right middle lobe (RML) pulmonary nodules, and a new right paracaval lymph node (1.1 cm). Nivolumab was discontinued and she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib 60 mg daily, with rationale including MET amplification (cabozantinib being a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor) and sarcomatoid histology.

During the early weeks on cabozantinib she experienced:
- Grade 2 hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR): painful erythema, hyperkeratosis, and blistering of bilateral palms and plantar surfaces, leading to dose reduction to 40 mg daily. Improved to Grade 1 (non-painful hyperkeratosis) within approximately 2 weeks of dose reduction.
- Grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools/day above baseline): managed successfully with loperamide and dietary modification, improving to Grade 1.
- Grade 2 hypertension (BP 158/96 mmHg): managed by uptitrating amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg daily; blood pressure controlled to Grade 1 level within 2 weeks.

Subsequent restaging scans at 3 months and 6 months on cabozantinib (the 6-month cabozantinib scan corresponding to approximately 15 months from original diagnosis) showed progressive disease stabilization and slow tumor reduction: the dominant RLL target lesion declined from 2.6 cm to 2.1 cm, the paracaval lymph node showed near-complete response (1.1 cm → 0.6 cm at 6 months), and all other lesions remained stable or slowly decreased.

At her prior visit (approximately 6 weeks ago), she reported new low back pain (Grade 1-2) over the preceding 4 weeks. MRI lumbar spine was performed and demonstrated multilevel degenerative disc disease (most pronounced L4-L5) without any evidence of vertebral metastases, pathologic fracture, or epidural disease. She was referred to physical therapy and started on naproxen (used cautiously given CKD stage 3a). Today she reports that back pain has improved substantially with these measures. She denies new or worsening neurological symptoms.

Today's visit includes review of restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed prior to this appointment.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: No fevers, chills, or night sweats. Fatigue rated 2/10 (mild, stable). Appetite good, weight stable per patient report (lost approximately 3 kg since original diagnosis, now approximately stable for the past 4 months).
Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with vigorous activity, unchanged from prior. No hemoptysis. No chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Intermittent loose stools (1-2 per day above her baseline), well-managed with PRN loperamide. No blood in stool. No nausea or vomiting. No abdominal pain. No mucositis or stomatitis currently.
Musculoskeletal: Substantial improvement in low back pain with physical therapy and as-needed naproxen. Residual mild stiffness in the mornings. No new bone pain. No joint pain or swelling.
Dermatologic: Persistent bilateral palmar and plantar hyperkeratosis (Grade 1 HFSR), non-painful and non-limiting at this time. No new blistering. Applying urea cream twice daily with good effect. No rash.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hypothyroidism (no excessive fatigue, constipation, cold intolerance, or hair loss beyond baseline). On levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. No palpitations, edema, or chest pain.
Genitourinary: Stable urinary frequency, no gross hematuria. No dysuria.
Neurological: No headaches, no focal neurological deficits, no cognitive changes.
Hematologic: No easy bruising or bleeding.
Other: No signs or symptoms of adrenal insufficiency or other new autoimmune phenomena.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (summarized, detailed in prior notes):
- Stage IV ccRCC, right kidney, as above
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled
- Hypertension
- Immune-related hypothyroidism (treatment-related, on levothyroxine)
- Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (solitary kidney post-nephrectomy, baseline creatinine ~1.4 mg/dL, eGFR ~46 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Mild lumbar spondylosis (degenerative disc disease L4-L5)
- Status post right radical nephrectomy with RPLND (cytoreductive, R0)

MEDICATIONS:
- Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (on empty stomach)
- Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
- Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
- Naproxen 375 mg orally twice daily PRN (back pain, with food; monitoring renal function)
- Loperamide 2 mg orally PRN (up to 8 mg/day for diarrhea)
- Urea 40% cream topically BID to hands and feet
- Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Retired registered nurse
- Non-smoker (lifelong)
- Occasional alcohol use (1-2 drinks per week, consistent with prior history)
- Nigerian-American; born in Lagos, Nigeria; immigrated to the United States approximately 35 years ago
- Lives with her husband; two adult children, one of whom accompanied her today
- Excellent social support
- Performs light gardening and daily walks; reports ability to walk approximately 20-30 minutes without significant limitation

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No family history of renal cell carcinoma or hereditary RCC syndromes
- Mother: hypertension, type 2 diabetes (deceased, cardiac disease)
- Father: unknown
- Germline testing: no pathogenic variants in VHL, FLCN, SDHB/C/D, MET, PTEN

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: Blood pressure 134/80 mmHg (left arm, sitting), Heart rate 72 bpm (regular), Respiratory rate 16/min, Temperature 36.8°C, O2 saturation 97% on room air, Weight 81.2 kg (BMI approximately 29.3 kg/m2 - stable)
ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished, Nigerian-American woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x4. Appears comfortable. Good affect. Adult child present at visit.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae, no conjunctival pallor. Oral mucosa moist, no mucositis or aphthous ulcers. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No pedal edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackles, or rhonchi. Adequate air entry throughout.
Abdominal: Soft, non-tender. Right flank scar from nephrectomy well-healed. No organomegaly palpable. No palpable lymphadenopathy in the inguinal or femoral regions. Bowel sounds present and normal.
Musculoskeletal: Mild paraspinal muscle tenderness in the lumbar region on palpation (L4-L5 distribution), improved from prior. No point tenderness over the vertebral spinous processes. Range of motion of lumbar spine: mildly limited in flexion and lateral bending due to stiffness, no reproducible neurological provocation. No hip tenderness. Upper and lower extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Gait steady and normal.
Dermatologic: Bilateral palmar hyperkeratosis, non-erythematous, without fissuring or blistering (Grade 1). Similar findings on plantar aspects of feet, bilateral, mild. No open wounds. No skin rash. No alopecia.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric bilaterally. Sensation to light touch and proprioception intact in upper and lower extremities. No signs of peripheral neuropathy.
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained within 48 hours prior to visit):
Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC: 2.8 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1, stable from prior)
  - Hematocrit: 32.1%
  - Platelets: 198 x10^9/L (normal)
  - MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 139 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  - Chloride: 104 mEq/L
  - Bicarbonate: 23 mEq/L
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL
  - Creatinine: 1.45 mg/dL (eGFR ~45 mL/min/1.73m2, stable, CKD stage 3a)
  - Glucose: 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated; consistent with known T2DM)
  - Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (corrected, normal)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  - AST: 28 U/L (normal)
  - ALT: 31 U/L (normal)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L (normal)
  - Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
  - LDH: 201 U/L (mildly elevated above ULN of 190; stable)

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH: 3.5 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 75 mcg; euthyroid)
  - Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Urinalysis:
  - Protein: trace (spot urine protein:creatinine ratio not elevated on concurrent check - <0.2)
  - No hematuria, no casts

HbA1c (checked at last visit): 7.1% (adequate glycemic control)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (restaging, reviewed today):
Target Lesion 1 (right lower lobe dominant pulmonary nodule): 2.0 cm, down from 2.1 cm at 6-month cabozantinib scan and 2.6 cm at cabozantinib initiation baseline. This lesion continues its slow but consistent downward trajectory.
Target Lesion 2 (right lower lobe second nodule): 0.4 cm, essentially unchanged.
Target Lesion 3 (left lower lobe nodule): 0.3 cm, stable to minimally decreased from 0.4 cm prior.
Right paracaval lymph node: no longer confidently measurable; near-complete response sustained.
Right middle lobe nodules (previously 0.6 and 0.4 cm, non-target): approximately 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm, stable to minimally decreased.
No new pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusion. No new intrathoracic adenopathy.
Abdomen/Pelvis: No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No new retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. No ascites. Postsurgical changes from right nephrectomy remain stable. No new masses.
Osseous survey (from CT): No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions. No pathologic fractures.
Sum of target lesion diameters: 2.7 cm (essentially unchanged from prior scan at 2.7 cm, and down from 3.7 cm at cabozantinib baseline — a sustained reduction of approximately 27% from TKI baseline).

Overall impression: Disease continues to look stable with sustained slow reduction in the dominant pulmonary target lesion. The retroperitoneal lymph node near-complete response is maintained. No new sites of disease. Encouraging continued disease control on cabozantinib.

Note: MRI brain was obtained approximately 3 months ago (at 6-month cabozantinib assessment) and showed no intracranial metastases. No dedicated brain imaging at today's visit; repeat brain MRI planned in approximately 3 months per routine surveillance.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. STAGE IV CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA — STABLE DISEASE ON CABOZANTINIB (9 MONTHS ON THERAPY)
Ms. Okonkwo has now been on cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg daily for approximately 9 months and continues to demonstrate durable disease control. Today's restaging CT shows sustained disease stability — the dominant RLL lesion continues its slow decline (now 2.0 cm, down from 2.6 cm at TKI start, representing an approximately 23% reduction), the paracaval lymph node near-complete response is maintained, and all remaining target lesions are stable. There are no new sites of disease.

The overall disease picture is encouraging. While she has not met formal partial response criteria (which would require a 30% or greater reduction in the sum of target diameters), she has had consistent, meaningful tumor shrinkage on virtually all measurable lesions, with the near-disappearance of the retroperitoneal nodal metastasis being particularly gratifying. Given the presence of 20% sarcomatoid differentiation and Fuhrman grade 4 histology — features that typically confer aggressive biology — maintaining stable disease for this duration represents a clinically meaningful outcome. The MET amplification on her NGS may be contributing to cabozantinib sensitivity given its dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitory mechanism.

Plan:
- Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. At this visit, the patient again defers dose re-escalation back to 60 mg, which is a reasonable decision given that her current Grade 1 HFSR has not fully resolved, and the 40 mg dose is providing excellent disease control. We will revisit this question at subsequent visits if HFSR completely resolves.
- Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in 3 months.
- Dedicated MRI brain in approximately 3 months (routine surveillance; last brain MRI was approximately 3 months ago, negative).
- Continue to monitor for emerging signs of progression at each visit and with interval imaging.

2. HAND-FOOT SKIN REACTION (HFSR) — GRADE 1, STABLE AND WELL-MANAGED
Grade 2 HFSR previously prompted dose reduction from 60 mg to 40 mg. On 40 mg, her HFSR has downgraded to Grade 1 (bilateral palmar and plantar hyperkeratosis, non-painful, non-limiting). Exam today confirms Grade 1 status only: dry, thickened skin without erythema, blistering, or fissuring.
Plan:
- Continue urea 40% cream twice daily to palms and plantar surfaces.
- Continue to use gel cushioned insoles and avoid prolonged standing or tight-fitting footwear.
- Reinforce skin care hygiene measures. Provide patient education handout on HFSR management.
- If Grade 1 fully resolves over the next cycle, may reconsider dose re-escalation at patient's preference.
- Dermatology follow-up as needed.

3. DIARRHEA — GRADE 1, INTERMITTENT, WELL-CONTROLLED
Intermittent 1-2 loose stools above baseline, PRN loperamide sufficient for control. No signs of colitis. Infectious etiologies excluded in prior workup; pattern consistent with TKI-related GI toxicity.
Plan:
- Continue PRN loperamide up to 8 mg/day.
- Advise low-residue diet during episodes.
- If diarrhea worsens to Grade 2 (4 or more stools above baseline, or lasting >2 days), patient instructed to call the oncology nursing line for management guidance.
- No dose reduction indicated at this time.

4. HYPERTENSION — CONTROLLED ON AMLODIPINE 10 MG
Blood pressure today 134/80 mmHg, well-controlled. Patient performs home BP monitoring twice daily as instructed. Amlodipine was uptitrated from 5 mg to 10 mg early in the cabozantinib course in response to Grade 2 hypertension.
Plan:
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily.
- Reinforce home BP monitoring. If BP consistently >140/90 mmHg, will add a second antihypertensive agent (ACE inhibitor or ARB would be preferred given renal protection benefit in CKD; however will use caution given solitary kidney).
- Patient instructed to report any headache, visual changes, or BP readings >160 mmHg systolic.

5. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE, STAGE 3a — STABLE
Creatinine 1.45 mg/dL, eGFR approximately 45 mL/min/1.73m2 — stable over the past several months. Expected in the setting of right radical nephrectomy (solitary kidney). No significant proteinuria on urine spot check.
Plan:
- Avoid nephrotoxic medications where possible. Continue cautious naproxen use for back pain with explicit instructions to use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration, and to hold if creatinine rises.
- Monitor creatinine and urine protein at each visit.
- Metformin continued at current dose with current renal function; will reassess if eGFR drops below 30 mL/min/1.73m2.
- No dose adjustment to cabozantinib required for current renal function.

6. LUMBAR DEGENERATIVE DISC DISEASE / BACK PAIN — IMPROVED
Back pain substantially improved with physical therapy and PRN naproxen. MRI lumbar spine confirmed degenerative disc disease (L4-L5) without metastatic disease. No focal neurological deficits on exam today.
Plan:
- Continue physical therapy.
- Use naproxen PRN at lowest effective dose with food, with close renal monitoring.
- Reinforce that new neurological symptoms (radiculopathy, leg weakness, urinary or bowel dysfunction) should prompt urgent evaluation.
- No bone-modifying agent indicated at this time (no osseous metastases identified).

7. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM — CONTROLLED ON LEVOTHYROXINE
TSH 3.5 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily — euthyroid. No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction.
Plan:
- Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (taken on empty stomach, at least 30 minutes before food or other medications).
- Recheck TFTs (TSH, free T4) at next visit.

8. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS — STABLE GLYCEMIC CONTROL
HbA1c 7.1% at last check; fasting glucose today 118 mg/dL. Well-controlled. Metformin 1000 mg BID continued.
Plan:
- Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily.
- Monitor HbA1c every 3-6 months.
- Reinforce dietary modification. No further medication adjustment needed at this time.

9. ANEMIA — GRADE 1, STABLE, CHRONIC
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, normocytic. Stable over the past several months. Multifactorial etiology: chronic disease of malignancy, prior surgical blood loss, and potentially cabozantinib-related (antiangiogenic agents can suppress erythropoiesis in some patients). On oral iron supplementation (initiated postoperatively; reviewed and continued).
Plan:
- No transfusion indicated. Continue current management.
- Monitor CBC at each visit.
- If hemoglobin falls below 9.0 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, will reassess for alternate etiologies and consider referral to hematology.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE
Ms. Okonkwo expresses overall satisfaction with her current treatment and quality of life. She has returned to light gardening and regular walking. FKSI-DRS (Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy — Kidney Symptom Index) scores have improved from prior assessment. She has a supportive husband and her adult children are involved in her care. No signs of depression or anxiety on today's assessment.
Plan:
- Continue to assess psychosocial well-being at each visit.
- Offer social work or counseling referral if patient requests.
- Encourage continuation of light physical activity within tolerance.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to clinic in approximately 3 months (or sooner if new symptoms arise). Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain at that time. Labs (CBC, CMP, TFTs) to be obtained within 48 hours prior to next visit.

Time spent: 55 minutes; greater than half of time devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
[Co-signed by Fellow/Resident]
